spans.content: The discrete Fourier transform is defined as a transformation on N complex numbers
z _ {k} \equiv \frac {1}{\sqrt {N}} \sum_ {j = 0} ^ {N - 1} y _ {j} e ^ {2 \pi i j k / N} \tag {20.1}
Note the analogy with the usual Fourier transform of a function
\tilde {f} (k) = \frac {1}{\sqrt {2 \pi}} \int d x f (x) e ^ {i k x} \tag {20.2}
where the sum on the discrete index
is replaced by an integral on the continuous
It is a unitary operation QFT defined on basis vectors
Q F T | j \rangle = \frac {1}{\sqrt {N}} \sum_ {k = 0} ^ {N - 1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k / N} | k \rangle \tag {20.3}
\begin{array} { r } { | v \rangle = \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } y _ { j } | j \rangle } \end{array}
given by the discrete Fourier transform (prove it). We will show
Using n qubits, the dimension N of the vector space of physical states is
The binary representation of the integer j is
Real numbers between 0 and 1 can be represented with binary fractions, defined by
and related to the binary integers
The discrete Fourier transform is defined as a transformation on N complex numbers
z _ {k} \equiv \frac {1}{\sqrt {N}} \sum_ {j = 0} ^ {N - 1} y _ {j} e ^ {2 \pi i j k / N} \tag {20.1}
Note the analogy with the usual Fourier transform of a function
\tilde {f} (k) = \frac {1}{\sqrt {2 \pi}} \int d x f (x) e ^ {i k x} \tag {20.2}
where the sum on the discrete index
is replaced by an integral on the continuous
It is a unitary operation QFT defined on basis vectors
Q F T | j \rangle = \frac {1}{\sqrt {N}} \sum_ {k = 0} ^ {N - 1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k / N} | k \rangle \tag {20.3}
\begin{array} { r } { | v \rangle = \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } y _ { j } | j \rangle } \end{array}
given by the discrete Fourier transform (prove it). We will show
Using n qubits, the dimension N of the vector space of physical states is
The binary representation of the integer j is
Real numbers between 0 and 1 can be represented with binary fractions, defined by
and related to the binary integers
Theorem: the unitary operator QFT can be written as the product

Q F T \left| j _ {1} j _ {2} \dots j _ {n} \right\rangle = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \left(\left| 0 \right\rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle\right) \left(\left| 0 \right\rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {n - 1} j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle\right) \dots \left(\left| 0 \right\rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {1} \dots j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle\right) \tag {20.7}

\begin{array}{l} Q F T | j \rangle = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \sum_ {k = 0} ^ {2 ^ {n} - 1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k / 2 ^ {n}} | k \rangle = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \sum_ {k _ {1} = 0} ^ {1} \dots \sum_ {k _ {n} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2 ^ {n} (k _ {1} 2 ^ {n - 1} + \dots + k _ {n} 2 ^ {0} ]} | k _ {1} \dots k _ {n} \rangle \\ = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \sum_ {k _ {1} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k _ {1} 2 ^ {- 1}} | k _ {1} \rangle \sum_ {k _ {2} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k _ {2} 2 ^ {- 2}} | k _ {2} \rangle \dots \sum_ {k _ {n} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k _ {n} 2 ^ {- n}} | k _ {n} \rangle \\ = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2} | 1 \rangle) (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2 ^ {2}} | 1 \rangle) \dots (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2 ^ {n}} | 1 \rangle) \tag {20.8} \\ \end{array}

```mermaid
graph LR
  subgraph Input_1["|j1⟩"]
  H --> R2 --> R3 --> Rn_1 --> Rn_1
  end

  subgraph Output_1["|j2⟩"]
  H --> R2 --> Rn_2 --> Rn_1
  end

  subgraph Intermediate_1["|j3⟩"]
  H --> R2 --> Rn_2 --> Rn_1
  end

  subgraph Output_2["|jn-1⟩"]
  H --> R2 --> Rn_2 --> Rn_1
  end

  subgraph Output_3["|jn⟩"]
  H --> R2 --> Rn_2 --> Rn_1
  end

  Rn_1 -.-> Rn_2
  Rn_2 -.-> Rn_2
  Rn_2 -.-> Rn_1
  Rn_1 -.-> Rn_2
  Rn_2 -.-> Rn_1
  Rn_1 -.-> Rn_2
  Rn_2 -.-> Rn_1
  Rn_1 -.-> Rn_2
  Rn_2 -.-> Rn_1
  Rn_1 -.-> Rn_2
  Rn_2 -.-> Rn_1
  Rn_1 -.-> Rm_2
  Rm_2 -.-> Rm_1
```
Theorem: the unitary operator QFT can be written as the product

Q F T \left| j _ {1} j _ {2} \dots j _ {n} \right\rangle = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \left(\left| 0 \right\rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle\right) \left(\left| 0 \right\rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {n - 1} j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle\right) \dots \left(\left| 0 \right\rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {1} \dots j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle\right) \tag {20.7}

\begin{array}{l} Q F T | j \rangle = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \sum_ {k = 0} ^ {2 ^ {n} - 1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k / 2 ^ {n}} | k \rangle = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \sum_ {k _ {1} = 0} ^ {1} \dots \sum_ {k _ {n} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2 ^ {n} (k _ {1} 2 ^ {n - 1} + \dots + k _ {n} 2 ^ {0} ]} | k _ {1} \dots k _ {n} \rangle \\ = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} \sum_ {k _ {1} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k _ {1} 2 ^ {- 1}} | k _ {1} \rangle \sum_ {k _ {2} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k _ {2} 2 ^ {- 2}} | k _ {2} \rangle \dots \sum_ {k _ {n} = 0} ^ {1} e ^ {2 \pi i j k _ {n} 2 ^ {- n}} | k _ {n} \rangle \\ = \frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {n}{2}}} (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2} | 1 \rangle) (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2 ^ {2}} | 1 \rangle) \dots (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i j / 2 ^ {n}} | 1 \rangle) \tag {20.8} \\ \end{array}

The above circuit implements the QFT on the n-qubit input state
Proof: after the first H gate, the input state becomes (prove it):
\frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {1} j _ {2}} | 1 \rangle) | j _ {2} \rangle \dots | j _ {n} \rangle \tag {20.12}
After the application of all controlled-R gates on the first qubit the state has become
\frac {1}{2 ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {1} j _ {2} \dots j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle) | j _ {2} \rangle \dots | j _ {n} \rangle \tag {20.13}
Applying the same procedure to the second qubit leads to:

\frac {1}{2} (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {1} j _ {2} \dots j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle) (| 0 \rangle + e ^ {2 \pi i 0. j _ {2} \dots j _ {n}} | 1 \rangle) | j _ {3} \rangle \dots | j _ {n} \rangle \tag {20.14}
and repeating it on all the other input qubits yields the final state that coincides
Q F T | j _ { 1 } j _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot j _ { n } \rangle
in (20.7), once we invert the order of the output
is unitary, since all gates are unitary. The circuit
gates. In contrast, the best classical
algorithms for the discrete Fourier transform use
an exponential advantage on its classical counterpart.
